meta.title: AQA GCSE English Language Mock Paper Bank
meta.subtitle: Original mock packs in the style of AQA GCSE English Language papers
notes: All sources, extracts and questions are original mock materials.
The bank is organised as complete paper packs so the dashboard can generate a full paper instead of a single isolated question.
A fallback copy of the bank is embedded in index.html so the dashboard still loads if JSON fetching fails.

questions.instructionsTop: Look in detail at Lines 4 to 8 of Source A.
questions.questionText: How does the writer use language to describe the harbour and Mara's feelings?
bulletPoints: You could include the writer's choice of words and phrases
You could include language features and techniques
You could include the effects on the reader

rubric.name: AO2 language analysis
rubric.notes: Reward clear explanation of language choices, precise references, and relevant effects.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: Harbour at Dawn
lines: The harbour did not appear all at once; it gathered itself out of the fog in pieces.
First came the bell, striking somewhere beyond the water.
Then Mara saw the black posts of the pier, beaded with rain.
Ropes knocked softly against wooden masts.
A gull swooped low enough to stir the mist above her head.
Everything smelled of salt, oil and wet rope.
She kept one hand in her pocket around the folded note.
It had become soft at the edges from being opened so often.
Ahead of her, a single lamp burned in the harbour office, pale and stubborn.
For a moment she wanted to turn back to the bus shelter and wait for daylight.
But the bell rang again, and the sound seemed less like a warning than a summons.
Mara lifted her case and walked on, straight into the bright circle of the lamp.

questions.instructionsTop: You now need to think about the whole of the source.
questions.questionText: How has the writer structured the source to interest you as a reader?
bulletPoints: You could write about what the writer focuses your attention on at the beginning
You could write about how and why the writer changes this focus as the source develops
You could write about structural features, shifts, contrasts and endings

rubric.name: AO2 structure analysis
rubric.notes: Reward understanding of structural methods and their effects across the source, supported by relevant references.

lines: The harbour did not appear all at once; it gathered itself out of the fog in pieces.

Then Mara saw the black posts of the pier, beaded with rain.

questions.instructionsTop: Look in detail at Lines 5 to 9 of Source A.
questions.questionText: How does the writer use language to present the fairground as both familiar and unsettling?
bulletPoints: You could include the writer's choice of words and phrases
You could include language features and techniques
You could include the effects on the reader

rubric.name: AO2 language analysis
rubric.notes: Reward clear explanation of language choices, precise references, and relevant effects.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: The Closed Fairground
lines: The locked gates of the fairground glittered with last night's rain.
Beyond them, the rides stood completely still.
Bright bulbs rimmed the ferris wheel, but none of them were lit.
A torn poster slapped against a kiosk in the wind.
The smell of damp wood and old sugar drifted through the railings.
Jonah pressed his face closer and peered between the bars.
He had not been back since the summer his brother won a goldfish here and dropped it on the walk home.
Even now, he could picture the tiny silver body flashing on the pavement.
At the centre of the fairground, one painted horse on the carousel had come loose and leaned at an awkward angle.
It looked less like a toy than a warning.
When a speaker crackled into life with a burst of static, Jonah jumped back so sharply that his elbow struck the gate.
Then the music stopped, and the fairground seemed to hold its breath.

questions.instructionsTop: You now need to think about the whole of the source.
questions.questionText: How has the writer structured the source to hold your interest?
bulletPoints: You could write about what the writer focuses your attention on at the beginning
You could write about how and why the writer changes this focus as the source develops
You could write about structural features, shifts, contrasts and endings

rubric.name: AO2 structure analysis
rubric.notes: Reward understanding of structural methods and their effects across the source, supported by relevant references.

questions.instructionsTop: Look in detail at Lines 5 to 8 of Source A.
questions.questionText: How does the writer use language to make Lina's descent into the basement seem tense and mysterious?
bulletPoints: You could include the writer's choice of words and phrases
You could include language features and techniques
You could include the effects on the reader

rubric.name: AO2 language analysis
rubric.notes: Reward clear explanation of language choices, precise references, and relevant effects.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: The Library Stairwell
lines: The public library had already closed when Lina noticed the light under the basement door.
Upstairs, every shelf stood in perfect shadow.
Only the returns desk glowed faintly from the streetlamp outside.
Dust drifted through the beam like ash in slow motion.
She should have gone home, but the light below seemed too deliberate to ignore.
Each stair creaked differently as she descended, as though the building were testing her step by step.
The air changed first: warmer, drier, carrying the faint smell of metal and paper glue.
Then she heard the steady turning of a page.
At the bottom of the stairwell she saw a room she had never known existed, lined with narrow cabinets and labelled boxes.
Someone sat at a desk inside, back turned, writing with a fountain pen.
Lina felt a thrill of discovery, followed immediately by the colder knowledge that she was not meant to be there.
The chair began to turn.

questions.instructionsTop: You now need to think about the whole of the source.

bulletPoints: You could write about what the writer focuses your attention on at the beginning
You could write about how and why the writer changes this focus as the source develops
You could write about structural features, shifts, contrasts and endings

rubric.name: AO2 structure analysis
rubric.notes: Reward understanding of structural methods and their effects across the source, supported by relevant references.

questions.instructionsTop: Look in detail at Lines 6 to 10 of Source A.
questions.questionText: How does the writer use language to show both damage and hope in this part of the source?
bulletPoints: You could include the writer's choice of words and phrases
You could include language features and techniques
You could include the effects on the reader

rubric.name: AO2 language analysis
rubric.notes: Reward clear explanation of language choices, precise references, and relevant effects.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: The Bridge After the Flood
lines: By noon, the river had dropped enough for the old bridge to show itself again.
Its stone back rose out of the brown water like the spine of some patient animal.
Branches and plastic bottles clung to the arches.
Mud striped the handrail where the flood had reached and then withdrawn.
Nadia crossed the square carrying a broom borrowed from the café.
Every doorway wore the same mark of yesterday's disaster.
Shopkeepers spoke quietly as they swept, as if loud voices might call the rain back.
When Nadia stepped onto the bridge, her boots left clear prints on the wet stone.
She stopped halfway to look at the fields beyond the village, flattened and shining under a sudden patch of blue sky.
For the first time since the storm began, she imagined what the place might look like repaired.
Someone behind her started to laugh: not because anything was funny, but because the bridge was still here.
A moment later, several more voices joined in.

questions.instructionsTop: You now need to think about the whole of the source.
questions.questionText: How has the writer structured the source to move from destruction towards recovery?
bulletPoints: You could write about what the writer focuses your attention on at the beginning
You could write about how and why the writer changes this focus as the source develops
You could write about structural features, shifts, contrasts and endings

rubric.name: AO2 structure analysis
rubric.notes: Reward understanding of structural methods and their effects across the source, supported by relevant references.

questions.instructionsTop: Look in detail at Lines 8 to 12 of Source A.
questions.questionText: How does the writer use language to present Imran's sense of duty in this part of the source?
bulletPoints: You could include the writer's choice of words and phrases
You could include language features and techniques
You could include the effects on the reader

rubric.name: AO2 language analysis
rubric.notes: Reward clear explanation of language choices, precise references, and relevant effects.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: The Winter Bus Depot
lines: Snow had frozen in thin ridges across the depot yard, turning tyre marks into pale grooves.
At that hour, the buses looked larger than usual, silent and blind behind frosted windows.
Orange light from the maintenance shed spread across the ground in a shallow fan.
Inside the office, a radio whispered the early news to no one.
Imran stood at the timetable board with his gloves still on.
He had checked the same cancelled route three times already, as if repetition might change the chalked word.
Behind him, a kettle clicked off.
The mechanic who usually joked through every winter morning said nothing at all.
Imran thought of the village at the end of the route, cut off again, and of the old woman who always waited with exact change and a shopping trolley.
He imagined the road under drifts, the hedges erased, the engine struggling uphill.
Then he reached for the marker pen and wrote a new departure time in the corner of the board.
It was a small act, but it felt like a promise.

questions.instructionsTop: You now need to think about the whole of the source.
questions.questionText: How has the writer structured the source to show Imran moving from hesitation to action?
bulletPoints: You could write about what the writer focuses your attention on at the beginning
You could write about how and why the writer changes this focus as the source develops
You could write about structural features, shifts, contrasts and endings

rubric.name: AO2 structure analysis
rubric.notes: Reward understanding of structural methods and their effects across the source, supported by relevant references.

questions.instructionsTop: You need to refer to both Source A and Source B for this question.
questions.questionText: Use details from both sources to write a summary of the different ways the writers experience time away from school.
bulletPoints: Write a summary of the differences and similarities
Use clear references to both sources
Focus on what each writer says about the topic

rubric.name: AO1/AO4 summary
rubric.notes: Reward clear, comparative summary using relevant references from both sources.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: School and Rest — modern text
lines: By the final bell, students should be able to shut their books and breathe.
Instead, many of us carry school in our pockets all evening through revision apps, alert messages and homework reminders.
A single missed notification can feel like a small disaster.
Teachers may not intend to create that pressure, but the constant stream of updates makes school feel endless.
I know classmates who check their phones during dinner in case a task has been changed.
There is a difference between encouraging hard work and creating an atmosphere in which rest feels irresponsible.
Young people need time to read for pleasure, help at home, play sport or simply be quiet.
If schools want resilient students, they should protect recovery time as carefully as lesson time.
Work matters, but so does the chance to step away from it.
Without that balance, learning becomes a treadmill rather than an education.

sourceB.label: Source B
sourceB.title: School and Rest — older text
lines: When I was twelve, the school day ended not with a message on a screen but with the slam of a wooden gate.
We ran from it as prisoners run from a cell, scattering into lanes, fields and back gardens.
No master could recall us by sending a warning to our pockets because our pockets contained marbles, not machines.
Homework existed, certainly, but it belonged to the table by the lamp after tea and never followed us to the riverbank.
Those loose hours taught us a second education.
We learned which trees bent in the wind and which streams could be crossed on one jump.
We argued, made up, invented rules and broke them.
By dusk we returned scratched, loud and gloriously tired.
It did not occur to any of us that idleness was shameful.
It was simply childhood doing its necessary work.

questions.instructionsTop: You need to refer to both Source A and Source B for this question.
questions.questionText: Use details from both sources to write a summary of what the seaside offers, and what problems it can create.
bulletPoints: Write a summary of the differences and similarities
Use clear references to both sources
Focus on what each writer says about the topic

rubric.name: AO1/AO4 summary
rubric.notes: Reward clear, comparative summary using relevant references from both sources.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: The Seaside — modern text
lines: On the first warm weekend of the year, our town fills as if someone has tipped out a box of bright pieces.
Buckets knock against knees, dogs drag their owners towards the beach and the queue at the chip shop forms before noon.
To visitors, this energy looks cheerful; to residents, it can feel like being gently invaded.
Parking spaces vanish, music escapes from every open car door and discarded wrappers collect in the grass by the promenade benches.
Yet by Monday morning, when the wind returns and the beach is empty apart from a tractor smoothing the sand, the place looks almost embarrassed by its own silence.
Seaside towns survive on visitors and resent them at the same time.
We complain about crowds all summer, then worry the moment the crowds disappear.
That contradiction is not hypocrisy; it is simply the economics of living somewhere beautiful.
Beauty attracts people.
People bring noise, mess and money.

sourceB.label: Source B
sourceB.title: The Seaside — older text
lines: I first saw the sea from the roof of the coach, standing on the seat because all the adults before me seemed suddenly to have grown taller.
The water flashed so hard in the sun that it looked like torn metal.
At once the whole party began shouting, not from fear but from a kind of delighted disbelief that such brightness could be real.
The town itself smelled of tar, salt and hot bread.
Ladies took the air beneath fluttering parasols while small boys ran towards the waves with the confidence of puppies.
Every stall appeared to promise some fresh wonder: shells, knives, printed cards, sweetmeats in jars.
I had never seen so much buying and laughing in one place.
To me, the seaside was abundance made visible.
Even the gulls seemed to cry out not for food but for excitement.
I thought no person could arrive there and remain dull.

questions.instructionsTop: You need to refer to both Source A and Source B for this question.
questions.questionText: Use details from both sources to write a summary of what travelling with other people is like for the writers.
bulletPoints: Write a summary of the differences and similarities
Use clear references to both sources
Focus on what each writer says about the topic

rubric.name: AO1/AO4 summary
rubric.notes: Reward clear, comparative summary using relevant references from both sources.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: Travelling to Work — modern text
lines: Every weekday at 7:42, the platform becomes a lesson in silent cooperation.
Strangers who would never greet one another in the street line up with extraordinary discipline at the exact points where the carriage doors are expected to open.
Coffee cups are clutched like passports.
Backpacks slide from shoulders without discussion.
When the train arrives late, a current of irritation moves through the crowd before anyone speaks.
Screens are checked, watches are raised, private plans are recalculated.
Commuting is often mocked as dull, but there is something impressive about the tiny acts of adjustment on which an entire city depends.
The morning journey is not glamorous, yet it is one of the clearest demonstrations that modern life only works because millions of people tolerate inconvenience together.
That patience has limits, of course.
One cancelled service is enough to turn stoicism into fury.

sourceB.label: Source B
sourceB.title: Travelling to Work — older text
lines: Our clerk insisted that the omnibus was a democratic machine, for it mixed banker, dressmaker, porter and widow upon equal terms, though not always upon equal cushions.
I rode on its roof for economy and for spectacle.
From that height, the city performed itself before me: apprentices sprinting with parcels, chimney smoke climbing into fog, a flower seller scolding a cabman as if she owned the street.
The horse, poor philosopher, proceeded with a patience superior to all parties concerned.
Whenever the vehicle lurched, strangers leaned into accidental fellowship.
One lady apologised each time the wheel struck a rut, as though she personally had designed the road.
Though the journey was slow, I preferred it to haste.
Delay offered observation, and observation is the true fare of a traveller.
By the time we reached the Strand, I felt I had not merely crossed the city but read a chapter of it.
No private carriage could have given me so public an education.

questions.instructionsTop: You need to refer to both Source A and Source B for this question.
questions.questionText: Use details from both sources to write a summary of why shared reading spaces matter to the writers.
bulletPoints: Write a summary of the differences and similarities
Use clear references to both sources
Focus on what each writer says about the topic

rubric.name: AO1/AO4 summary
rubric.notes: Reward clear, comparative summary using relevant references from both sources.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: Libraries and Community — modern text
lines: The easiest way to misunderstand a library is to imagine that it is mainly about books.
Books matter, of course, but in many towns the library is one of the last indoor places where you are welcome to stay without proving you intend to spend money.
Teenagers revise there because home is noisy.
Parents take toddlers there because flat walls are kinder than small flats.
Older visitors read newspapers there because conversation at home has become a rare event.
The modern library lends heat, light, tables, silence and company as much as it lends novels.
When funding is cut, officials often talk as if they are trimming a luxury.
They are not.
They are weakening a piece of social infrastructure that people notice most clearly only after it disappears.
A closed library does not merely remove shelves; it narrows a community's idea of who belongs in public.

sourceB.label: Source B
sourceB.title: Libraries and Community — older text
lines: In the winter of my apprenticeship, I discovered the mechanics' institute reading room and entered it first from cold, though I continued to return from ambition.
The room was plain enough: long tables, straight chairs, a coal fire too small for comfort and newspapers fastened to poles like reluctant flags.
Yet to me it possessed the dignity of a cathedral.
Men who laboured all day with iron filings in their cuffs bent there over atlases, manuals and speeches.
No one asked whether a blacksmith's son had business with history.
The very existence of the room answered that question on my behalf.
I learned as much from the silence of the place as from the print.
It suggested that self-improvement was not a joke or a private fantasy but an honourable public activity.
When I left each evening, the street appeared narrower than before, as if the room had enlarged me.
I owe to it not my trade, perhaps, but the appetite to outgrow it.

questions.instructionsTop: You need to refer to both Source A and Source B for this question.
questions.questionText: Use details from both sources to write a summary of how difficult weather affects communities.
bulletPoints: Write a summary of the differences and similarities
Use clear references to both sources
Focus on what each writer says about the topic

rubric.name: AO1/AO4 summary
rubric.notes: Reward clear, comparative summary using relevant references from both sources.

sourceA.label: Source A
sourceA.title: Weather and Community — modern text
lines: The weather app on my phone declared the storm over before the town had even finished counting the damage.
Forecasts are tidy; aftermath is not.
By breakfast, neighbours were already outside, stepping around fallen branches and comparing roof tiles as if discussing exam results.
The road by the post office had become a shallow stream.
One shopkeeper set out a bucket to catch water leaking through a fresh crack in the ceiling and then opened anyway.
Extreme weather is often described in dramatic images, but what stays with me is its administrative side: forms, queues, missed deliveries, borrowed generators, shared kettles.
Storms test not only buildings but habits.
Do people check on one another?
Do they share tools, time and information?
That is where a community's real forecast is written.

sourceB.label: Source B
sourceB.title: Weather and Community — older text
lines: After the great snowfall, our lane became less a road than a negotiation.
Gates vanished, hedges rounded themselves into white sculpture and every familiar boundary grew uncertain.
For two days no cart reached us.
This inconvenience, which would have offended me in autumn, delighted me in January because it transformed neighbours into allies.
The baker's boy arrived on foot and was applauded as if he were an explorer.
A farmer lent his horse to pull free a trapped carriage and received six contradictory instructions and one heartfelt cheer.
Children, released from ordinary usefulness, carried messages between houses with the self-importance of diplomats.
Snow had isolated us, yes, but it had also obliged us to notice one another.
By the thaw, I almost regretted the return of independence.
Comfort makes us efficient; difficulty makes us visible.
